content.problemStatement:
Alice has NNN coins. The value of ithi^{th}ith coin is CiC_iCi​. Bob wants to buy a toy whose value is XXX. Alice, being a good friend of Bob, agrees to lend enough money to Bob so that he can buy the toy. However, Alice wants to lend as few coins as possible to Bob. Determine the minimum number of coins Alice can lend to Bob such that Bob can buy his desired toy. Bob can buy his desired toy if he can get coins whose combined worth is greater than or equal to XXX.

If there exists no way for Alice to lend Bob coins such that Bob can buy his desired toys, print "-1".


Input Format

First line will contain a single integer TTT, the number of test cases. Then TTT test cases follow.
Each test case will contain two lines of input.
The first line of each test case contains two integers NNN and XXX.
The second line of each test case contains NNN integers, A1,A2...ANA_1, A_2 . . . A_NA1​,A2​...AN​.


Output Format

Print a single integer denoting the minimum number of coins which Alice must lend to Bob so that he can buy his desired toy. If there exists no way for Alice to lend Bob coins such that Bob can buy his desired toys, print "-1" (without quotes).
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases ≤ 3·10^5
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines. The first line of a test case contains two integers N and X — the number of coins Alice has and the price of the toy. The second line contains N space‑separated integers C1, C2, …, CN, the values of the coins.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the minimum number of coins Alice must lend so that the total value is at least X. If it is impossible, output -1.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1: The coin 7 (or 8) alone reaches the required value, so the answer is 1.\nTest case 2: The smallest number of coins whose sum ≥ 7 is 2 (e.g., 2+5 or 3+4).

solution.approach: All coin values are positive. To minimise the number of coins whose sum reaches at least X we should always take the largest available coins first. Therefore, for each test case we sort the array of coin values in non‑increasing order, then iterate from the biggest value, accumulating a running sum until it becomes ≥ X. The number of taken coins is the answer. If the total sum of all coins is still < X, the answer is -1.
solution.timeComplexity: Sorting dominates: O(N log N) per test case. Over all test cases O((ΣN) log N_max).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) for storing the coin values of a test case.

code.Python3:
import sys

def solve():
    data = sys.stdin.read().strip().split()
    if not data:
        return
    it = iter(data)
    T = int(next(it))
    out_lines = []
    for _ in range(T):
        N = int(next(it)); X = int(next(it))
        coins = [int(next(it)) for _ in range(N)]
        coins.sort(reverse=True)
        s = 0
        cnt = 0
        for v in coins:
            s += v
            cnt += 1
            if s >= X:
                break
        out_lines.append(str(cnt) if s >= X else '-1')
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out_lines))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    solve()
